page_content: >> Hey adapters, joining me is Steven Weinstein. Stephen is the CEO of Mangrove Property Insurance. Hi, Stephen. Welcome to the podcast. >> It's great to be here with you. >> I've covered Florida on my podcast quite a bit. I'm actually from Florida originally. I'm not living there right now. And obviously with all the hurricanes and all the extreme weather events, it's a challenging place to do insurance. Can you give my listeners sort of an overview? What is the insurance situation like in Florida right now? across my career and I know you're a veteran and a resident a longtime resident of the market but really we've had two challenges in the Florida property market over the last 30 years. One is a natural crisis, a climate driven challenge and one was a man-made crisis. The eternal challenge of Florida is managing risk in a sea level peninsula that juts out into very warm water with high density of high value properties.

But the other set of risks was entirely self-inflicted, a man-made crisis that manifested itself over 30 decades with a series of apparent many crises, but all had their roots in the same unique set of attributes that in an American context were unique to Florida. Florida did not follow the American rule in a legal perspective. For example, with Florida had a regime of oneway attorney's fees. Florida had a bad faith standard which was not harmonized with the rest of the country. Florida had a punitive damage standard which was not harmonized with the rest of the United States. Florida for property claims that are visible fairly quickly had a multi-year reporting tale whereas in the rest of the US and in the EU countries that's a one-year tail. And Florida had permitted the weaponization of something that we call in the trade assignment of benefits which have their purpose across insurance. It's how your pharmacist gets paid on in respect of your insurance.

But Florida had permitted a weaponization of what we call AOB that contributed to a series of continuing crises and that played out for decades. We had a sinkhole crisis which managed to disappear geologically as soon as we passed a targeted sinkhole reform. We had a mold crisis which dried up as soon as the industry passed a targeted mold reform. We had a first water crisis and a second water crisis. We had a Chinese drywall crisis. Really what was at the root of all those crises over time was the set of unique attributes in Florida. In a series of legal and regulatory reforms that culminated in the first half of last year, Florida's now harmonized its standards in respect of those challenges with the rest of the country. Nothing particularly radical has occurred. We've really just taken Florida standards and aligned them with the way that people operate in Massachusetts and New York and Texas and South Carolina.

And that lets us get back to the real business of helping homes and families and small businesses be safer from the real risk in the world, which in a Florida context is hurricanes. But that's been such an important and unique issue of the really distinctive issue of the Florida market of the last three decades. And this transformative set of reforms is perhaps the most exciting thing we've seen in the markets across that time. give us a little bit of information on Citizens, which is the state-run, and I I don't know if that's act the best way to describe that, but they were supposed to be the kind of the insurance of last resort, but that's changed quite a bit. And look at you. You're a relatively new insurance company. What What's citizens doing right now? >> Yeah. For your listeners, let me step all the way back, too. And there's a lot to be thoughtful about in Florida, but also a lot to applaud.

And something that I've always tried to salute is that Florida over these years has really developed and stuck to even when it's challenging a culture of insurance. And while we've had pros and cons, the way this has manifested over time, in Florida in general, people have had access to insurance and the insurance has covered their most significant exposure, which has been hurricane. For example, on the federal level, and we've seen this in the news recently, most Americans have access to flood coverage but don't have flood coverage. What we call the takeup rate, which is jargon for the percentage of homeowners who elect when it's voluntary, flood coverage, even when it's subsidized, is around 9%. California has been the news with a range of challenges, including wildfire.

But California's real peak risk is in range in automain earthquake and Californians have access to subsidized earthquake coverage, but on a voluntary basis, the takeup rate for California quake is 9%. is about the same number what Americans will elect to take in terms of coverage for risks that they perceive as remote and if Florida we've had something different whether in the private market with companies like Mangarrove or through as you've mentioned citizens flidians with a mortgage or otherwise have had insurance and the insurance covers their most significant exposure flood aside which is has been windstormed and citizens is a state sponsored entity which provides primary insurance the concept design of citizens has always been to make it available as a option of last resort. And at times of crisis, the policy can the citizens will swell up.

That 30-year game of whack-a-ole that I tried to describe finally culminated in a the most significant iteration of that dynamic that we had seen. And it caused more than a dozen Florida domestic primary companies to go out of business and be unable to write new business. And in those dynamics, the number of Florida residents who had no option other than Florida citizens swelled. And Florida citizens went from call it half a million policies which is a pretty big company to something heading towards three times that size with the reforms and with the strong leadership team that's there at Citizens. Citizens has been looking to derisk depopulate and return policies that can be underwritten and protected in the private market back to the private market. And I think that's a healthy dynamic. It does open up opportunities for companies like Mangarov and the many companies that we compete with.

And it lets citizens focus their executive time, focus their capital on serving Florida residents for whom citizens is perhaps the only option perhaps as a function of the nature of the home, its location, and the ability of the current resident to pay an actuarily sound rate for their home at their location. Citizens will always play an important role. But it's a funny thing in the respect that citizens is stronger when it's a little bit smaller and the market is healthier when there are policies in the private market investing in private market capital solutions including reinsurance and we've been seeing much more of that over the last year. >> We actually met at the climate connect conference and just a couple questions here related to that. First off, kudos to you, but why did you decide to attend this climate conference? And this is very I'm very curious about this.

Are your customers even thinking about issues like climate change, this big issue like climate change when they're buying property insurance? >> I can't necessarily put myself in the minds of our customers, but but in Florida, we're also aware of the risks of windstorms and the risks of hurricanes and the risk of warmer water and and changes that we all can see in the nature of precipitation. I think we all want to be more risk aware and we all want to invest in the safety of our families and I think Mangro for sure wants to understand the risk that we're underwriting and that we're protecting and that we're placing in global reinsurance markets as robustly as we can and we want to help our policy holders with the tools that will help them to be most more physically safer from the next storm and more financially resilient. We don't understand anything in this world perfectly.

One of the things that we understand reasonably well, it's been studied very robustly, is the risk of US southeast hurricanes, Florida storms, and what happens in a physical market like Florida when wind and water hit structures. But we can absolutely understand it better. And the more that we lean in, continue to collect data and analyze objectively, always trying to learn from prior events and thinking about what current trends will pose us in the future is healthy. That was a fun event for me to be at. I thought that dialogue was healthy. And the more that we can all lean into a culture of risk awareness and risk understanding and humility and the more that we can collaborate with thoughtful people and strong institutions that are promoting resilience, I think the better off we'll all be. I guarantee my listeners are thinking this. And so you'd mentioned these reforms that have happened in Florida.

But again, when the next big storm hits, let's say really hits a lot of your customers really hard. People are thinking, are you able to weather the claims that might come after a big think of Fort Myers that they got hit in the last few years? How are you different? What's going on with Mangrove that because that's inevitably going to happen where these storms are going to hit and how are you going to be able because so many of those insurance companies just they went bankrupt. across time and I've been around the Florida market for three decades as a reinsurer, as someone who's helped sponsor some of the companies that I've participated now in my role as the CEO of Mangrove. As I've noted on this call, the world peak risk is Florida hurricane.

And what we mean by that is if you had an insurance company that covered the whole world in every peril in the world, the single peril and location that would absorb the most of your capital that would be the most expensive for you to hold would be Florida hurricane. And that's driven by a couple of things. Florida hurricanes are significant events. Earthquakes in California, in Japan, and other places that are seismically active, those can be very severe, but we don't expect the same amount of frequency that we expect in Florida. Supertorm Sandy was a significant event, but we don't expect the same amount of frequency in the US northeast as we expect in the southeast and particularly Florida. And there are regions of the world with a lot of meteorological tropical storms, but not the population density and insured property values that we see in Florida.

And that set of features taken together, the severity of Florida storm risk, the expected potential frequency, and the value of insured properties makes Florida hurricane by far the world peak risk. The magic of insurance is diversification. We make a pool of life insurance risk, of auto policies, of homeowners policies more affordable by diversifying the exposures. It's the magic of what we do. And the book of business that I've built up in Florida since our launch this year has well has much in common because we focused on strong construction and good risks. But we have diversity by location, by the particular habits of our policy holders. But in a storm, we would run the risk of concentration.

Our partners in the global reinsurance business can provide that diversification and it's where the world peak risk is most efficiently financed by exporting our concentrated catastrophe exposed hurricane risk to what's in general a ready willing enabled global reinsurance market while promoting some things that are if not unique but strong about us alignment in the originator of the risk which is mangrove that's how we can drive efficiency and ultimately affordability down the policyholder But in the meantime, I can assure you a mangrove policy holder can sleep at night. We've invested at record setting levels in reinsurance, which is the ecosystem from which I come. I was extremely gratified by the support from Mangrove and global reinsurance markets. Every company operating in Florida invests in reinsurance. I hope that people invest at the same levels of protection and I think my competitors generally do that Mangrove has.

And it's that mix of reinsurance protection and internal balance sheet strength that over time ensure that claims get paid. And Doug, I'll touch on as well. The strongest signal we have in some respects about the risk of storms is the price signal that we get from the global reinsurance market. And I think people recognize that the transformative legislative forums have taken some of the I'll just call it the fraud risk down from where it has been. But there's no active man and woman that we can wave that will change materially the risks of storms. We can build more resiliently. We can make sure our communities have the resources to recover rapidly, but we're not going to make the next hurricane less likely. The best thing that we can do is be prepared to be maximize the option value of these companies by assuring our capital partners that we'll provide that will survive.

But we need the raw materials to invest in reinsurance and risk transfer so that we can deliver that value that I think we've touched on to assure our policyh holders, our stakeholders, everyone that depends upon us that every promise that is made by Mangrove and our competitors in the system that those promises will be kept. >> I want you to speculate just a little bit here. So look out 10 years in the Florida property insurance market. And so I grew up in Sarasota, Florida and I go back there frequently and I have a family friend in a very wellto-do neighborhood on the water and they have decided to forego property insurance even though this property is worth a lot and I thought that was very interesting.

Just speculate 10 years from now are we going to see a lot more of that or are there going to be I guess businesses like yours that are really going to be able to offer an affordable and this is generally someone who could probably afford it and they're still deciding to forego property insurance. Well, let me pause just there with self-interest noted. I I hope that no one elects to forego their insurance >> and this is happening. This is happening though this >> even if you have the perception that you can. So from a the perspect from a Vulcan's perspective it's a very efficient use of your money even if you think you have the options to do different things. I relish buying reinsurance on a personal level. I like buying my life insurance. I am an insurance nerd and I don't regret at the end of the year when I haven't collected on my life insurance policy. It's a valuable product.

It's a very efficient investment and it certainly makes our family dynamic, more resilient should what I hope is a remote contingency occur. What I hope we see over time is a market that remains healthy. I believe that these reforms will be stable. I think it's incumbent upon all of us to communicate the efficacy that they have brought to the market, the stability of carriers that I think is uh we are seeing the return of new competition. Mangro is not the only new company in Florida this year by any means. I think we're up to 14 or 15 new entrance over this period of time. So, my own forecast is we'll see a a healthy, stable market with robust competition and not knowing the structure of your content and that more and more slowly and steadily the built environment of Florida will continue to be more resilient. Something we're very focused on at Mango is that when we have an opportunity to help our policy holders rebuild, we help them rebuild better.

And I think over time this it has been a focus of the state government to invest in resilience to support programs like my safe Florida home which give policyh holders the option and the opportunity to invest in their own physical safety. Every year we make a step forward. I hope in 10 years we'll have made a very significant stride forward. So if my listeners want to learn more about your company, what do you recommend? >> Visit our website mangrofl.com. If you are Flidian resident interested in insurance, we are an agent focused market as well, but your best resource frequently is your independent insurance agent. They know your community well and reach out to your local insurance industry and explore all your choices.

And to learn more about pragmatic, actionable tips that you can take now to make your home safer, to make your family more resilient from the Naval Storm, I'd encourage you to visit the Institute for Business and Home Safety, ibhs.org, or where tips that you can take that your contractor can take. It's the time to make your home more resilient for the next event is always now and there are things that we all can do to ensure that our family is a little bit safer, a lot safer and then also as well that your home is a more attractive risk to underwrite. But uh we are all the heroes of our own resilience journey and I'd encourage your listeners to educate themselves as much as they can and do things that they pragmatically can.

One of them is investing insurance but physical enhancements to your home is a really important part of our show. journey and I encourage everyone to be thoughtful about it. >> I have a lot of listeners that aren't from Florida, but I'm sure they'd be interested in what you're up to because we're very wonky and policy oriented. So, they'd probably be curious on how you're doing things there. Stephen, it's

>> Hey adapters, joining me is Francis Buchard. Francis is the managing director of climate at Marsh Mlennon. Marsh McClennon is the world's leading professional services firm in risk, strategy, and people. Hi Francis, welcome back to the podcast. >> Hey Doug, it's great to be back. >> Always a treat to chat with you. But first off, for those listeners who didn't hear you on the previous episode you were on, what is Marsh McClennon and what do you do there? >> So Marsh McClennon is a company that has four functional operational units.

One is Marsh itself and that's the world's largest broker commercial broker primarily but they also operate in the US under MMA Marsh Mlan agency so they've got a large agency force then you've got Guy Carpenter which is the world's largest reinsurance broker they really help the insurance companies understand their portfolios and how to buy or what to buy from reinsurance or alternative markets or self retentions captives that kind of stuff Francis I'm having you on because I've had some really great conversations with you and you are really thinking big and on this issue of insurance and climate change and I want to get your perspective but a lot of things are going on here and so let's start big picture and how did the insurance system get so fragile in the face of climate risk and were there signals out there that were being missed or was it just a natural progression why we're we're at where we're at a little bit of everything there were voices in the '9s that were starting to say look we got to get our arms around the science here I know that because the primary voice was my boss us at the time, Frank Nutter, and he was spending a lot of time with the Noah and NASA and other government agencies that really had an early finger on the pulse of the data that was coming in to show some pretty fundamental shifts in climatic trends.

I I don't think it's a new topic. I don't think it's one that, you know, was missed. you go back and read, you know, literature from Munich Re or Swiss Re or other companies that have been active in the space for a long time, you'll see a lot of papers, a lot of reports, a lot of data from the '90s and the odds around this topic in the need to understand the connection between carbon emissions and not just climate natural disasters, both the frequency and the scale. So, I don't think it's a new topic that kind of came out of left field and surprised people. Doug, I I don't really feel it that way. What I do think is that the industry has struggled to come up with a coherent approach to these jurisdictions where the risk is going much is increasing and we're struggling to keep up with price whether that's from regulatory or judicial system or simply customer preference.

So I think it's been one that's been more of what it appears and it's bit more like whack-a-ole and we've been able to make our way through with the regulatory issues and sometimes it's structural changes we've made to be able to provide coverage in Florida for example or we found other devices to be able to still serve our clients and our markets despite what is clearly an increasing risk. I think it's just getting to the point now where the price point is starting to be felt at the consumer level and it's really starting to raise questions. So, not only on the price, but the frequency. It seems every week there's a new natural disaster. I think it I do not think that the industry is by any means that proverbial frog in the boiling pot that we didn't know was happening until that's not the case whatsoever. I think it's more where where we've been hustling to solve the issue here, solve it there, address it here, find a new way to to use sublims or the excess markets.

So, I think we found kind of solutions to the issues as they've come up, but now it's getting harder to do that. All right. So, you've worked in public affairs and private reinsurance, and I hate to admit it, but I actually didn't really quite know what reinsurance was until relatively recently, last few years. And so what's the average person missing about the role of reinsurance and the role it plays in their coverage? So I'm going to go back to my days lobbying for the reinsurance association to use some of my basic descriptions. The one I think is most effective is if is the difference between you and your insurance company. And what I mean by that is you as an individual don't have the probably don't most people don't have the financial resources to ensure themselves for all the risks that they face in a modern economy. So you find a willing partner who for a price is willing to assume some of the exposure and liability that that you face just in in day-to-day life.

And that insurance company actually knows better than you do. You know what your exposure is. So they can craft a a policy or a product that actually meets the needs that that you face. An insurance company is in a similar position. and it knows its portfolio but it needs third-party support to understand evolving or emerging risks portfolio broader portfolio. So keep in mind the reinsurance companies get to see many company portfolios. So they have a even more macro understanding of trends and they see them earlier so they can inform their clients of trends. They can provide their clients capital to either expand in markets or to shore up their reserves. they can get access to non-traditional capital if they want to tap into cap bonds or the ILS market or whatnot. So the reinsurers are that higher level professional risk managers helping insurance companies just as the insurance company is helping you. Some people just say it's the insurance company for insurance companies.

It's deeper than that because of the advice and the portfolio level insights that the reinsurers provide. It goes much beyond that. >> All right. No, that's fantastic. I just like grounding people in that because reinsurance obviously is going to be playing a bigger role in the years ahead.

Now I want to pivot a bit here and let's talk about your work in Los Angeles after the big wildfires and I think you've been taking the red eye from the east coast to the west coast and what is the Delta fund and how does it reflect a new kind of post- disaster insurance model and I just want to say you wrote a piece for the epicenter and that was really fantastic. you really described in detail like this whole notion of a new model and tell us a bit about that the Delta fund and all those things happening. >> Yeah, I appreciate that and I guess I'd start off by saying it's been really encouraging to hear and to see and to be part of some of the discussions that are happening in Los Angeles right now. It's devastation is everywhere and it's just awful what happened there clearly. But there's a spirit that's truly innovative out there.

And in some cases, it's what the what necessity is the motherhood of innovation is mother of innovation because this could be the first post FEMA or no FEMA type of large-scale recovery in America. So they know they're going into this alone and that seems to have sparked tenacity with a lot of the leaders out there and a lot of different neat innovative ideas are popping up. The Delta Fund, the one you referenced, is one that really appeals to me because like you Doug and the army of which we're part of because there are a lot of us saying this and doing this, but I've been trying to raise awareness around the insurance sector's need to really find a way to accelerate the resilient building or retrofits or we've got to reduce the risk that needs be that's really the only sustainable solution to some of these markets that are facing increased risk due to climate issues.

So, the Delta Fund is the first initiative that I've heard of or been part of where we're going to try to amass a significant amount of money, probably around $250 million. Some of it will be philanthropic. Some of it will be return bearing. And we're going to try to provide all 12,000 homeowners who have to rebuild at the Palisades and Altadena. Give them the option to build to a higher above the 7A or the standard building code that that exists today in California. This is essentially a fund to implement IBHS standards in a massive recovery operation like we're looking at there in in California. And whether it goes well or it doesn't go well, it's going to be a fascinating opportunity to learn, Doug, because we're going to be giving low-income people, high-income people, owners who have lived there forever, owners who are just brand new. It's going to be a real diverse set of homeowners choosing whether they want resilience and having a fund that will facilitate it.

And we've never really been able to do that at scale. And our hope is that obviously we will not only attract a lot of homeowners to to tap into the fund and finance the resilient rebuild, but that we'll also be able to work with the communities and and instill incentives so that the individual homeowner efforts are complemented by community efforts. And then and this is where the kicker is, Doug, if we can do this well and we can track what worked and what didn't, then we can take this national, this concept national, right? There's no need for the insurance sector to be rebuilding homes after disasters in the exact same fashion they were built before. We need to start to lead in this kind of a crusade perhaps of resilience where you know every house that we touch should be rebuilt to a higher resilient standard if that if the local exposure requires it. So, we've got a fund.

We're trying to embed it into that that infrastructure of construction firms, contractors, banks, insurers, insurance agents, the government itself, different nonprofits. So, the point is we want there to be multiple entry points into this fund. So, wherever someone's going for advice, they should be hearing about this fund and having the option to buy into it. When you think of the next generation of insurance customers and they might demand something very different, more transparency, more climate consciousness, but are customers really that sophisticated to start making those decisions anytime soon. We hear that it's like, all right, and they want to factor in climate change, but are we anywhere close to that? >> Doug, I think if you had asked me 5 days ago, I may have said no. >> Okay. I got to Colorado a few days ago and one of the advertisements between the periods of the Stanley Cup hockey game was a an ad for fire retardant materials to build decks with.

So they were talking about the WOOI standards and all it takes to keep keep home safe. And I had never seen anything like that before. Now back on the East Coast, maybe wildfire isn't as prominent, but that tells me that resilience is becoming a selling point. And I think consumers are starting to recognize that there are steps they can take that reduce their risk and will ultimately reduce their insurance prices. That this is not a FA accomp, okay, I live here. There's nothing I can do. I'll just see where it goes. No, there are a lot of things homeowners can do. And the fact that we're starting to see people advertise to differentiate themselves based on resilience standards and fire standards that it takes an engineer to understand, I think that's a signal. And I'm not suggesting it suggests we're we're there yet. Uh but I do think that there's a growing awareness of these issues and the connection of these issues.

I I sometimes I tell a joke when I give speeches or I think it's a joke. I try to tell a joke. I don't know if it's funny or not, but I tell people to go to a local bar and wait for someone from Florida to show up because they will show up and then ask them about their insurance and they will give you the details of the insurance market like a professional agent. Doug, these people have become so by necessity so well informed about risk and risk options and insurance options and the excess market and umbrellas and all these other things that I do think the consumers are getting more sophisticated because the issue is becoming more of a pocketbook issue and I think that will only go in one direction in terms of the knowledge base. All right, I want you to have a little fun with this final question. Let's jump ahead to 2035. what does a functioning climate aare insurance system look like? And maybe give us some advice on how to get there and be aspirational if you want.

Now, let's not be cynical because a lot of things could go south because of the government not being involved, whatever. But if it we really were on the right path. What help us visualize that? >> Yeah, I actually wrote a piece on exactly that a few years ago. So, maybe I'll send that to you. But it was really a combination of things. one was a national fund, not too different from the Delta fund we're talking about here, that was really intended both to drive retrofits, but also to to support communities as they implemented risk reduction initiatives. So, I'm talking about a massive fund that was return bearing. This is not charitable or philanthropic, but a return bearing fund that was out there facilitating transactions and d-risking transactions both at the homeowner level and at the community level.

So you had money dedicated, not grant money that was trickled out for political purposes, but real money that was there to support a transformation in the country and from a maybe a an unknown risk to a knowing your risk and doing something about it. But there was also, I would argue, we'd have some type of red zone building metric where we could see how much how much homes were still being built or not being built in clear danger zones. We'd have, you know, a core of individuals, whether it's similar to the Americanore or the Teachers Corps or the Peace Corps or whatever. We'd have people out there who maybe weren't ready to jump into the workforce and wanted to work outdoors or work with their bodies instead of their minds yet to go out there and build the nature-based solutions out there, restore the swamps. We So, we'd have the the workforce to implement what the money was hiring.

We'd have new technologies and the promise about IoT and the internet of everything, internet of things would would have been delivered at a home level where a home can really almost encase itself in a protective electronic sphere bubble or something like that where it knows all the risks that are occurring in that home and it's taking steps to stop them. The regulations would have changed. Some of the tax issues probably would have to change.

So it the real aspirational magic I think Doug is a system that's fully aligned because right now we're all running generally in the same direction but a lot of us are different speeds and some are going at angles and some are going straight and some are going in circles and there there's just so much misalignment across systems right now that everything is full of friction and I think the to think forward 2035 I think the answer is a truly aligned Not just disaster recovery system, but a disaster preparedness, urban planning, land use planning, tax, all these systems right now don't work together and they all need to in order for us to really get this risk under control and be able to have sustainable insurance markets for the long term.
